comments.author: @WhereonEarth4K
comments.text:
👇 Everything we curated for this Angkor episode. Tours, books, cookbooks, travel resources.

*📚 BROWSE THE ANGKOR COLLECTION*
  ◦ Every book, cookbook, and cultural item we'd recommend for this episode → https://amzn.to/4vUSVTJ

*🛍 SEE OUR FULL W.O.E. STOREFRONT*
  ◦ Every destination Collection in one place → https://www.amazon.com/shop/whereonearth

*🎟 TOURS AND EXPERIENCES IN ANGKOR*
  ◦ Sunrise tours of Angkor Wat and the temple complex → https://shorturl.at/6Xuam
  ◦ Browse all Angkor and Siem Reap experiences → https://shorturl.at/yEMwq

*📖 PLAN YOUR ANGKOR TRIP*
  ◦ Lonely Planet Cambodia, the current edition guide to Siem Reap and the temples of Angkor → https://amzn.to/4gsIEcU
  ◦ Lonely Planet Southeast Asia Phrasebook and Dictionary, with Khmer for your trip → https://amzn.to/3SN6fLw

*📚 GO DEEPER INTO ANGKOR*
  ◦ Angkor and the Khmer Civilization by Michael Coe and Damian Evans, the archaeologist whose laser survey opens this story → https://amzn.to/4a6YbLJ
  ◦ In the Shadow of the Banyan by Vaddey Ratner, a PEN/Hemingway finalist novel by a survivor of the Khmer Rouge → https://amzn.to/44izaJW

*🍲 BRING ANGKOR HOME*
  ◦ NHUM, Recipes from a Cambodian Home Kitchen by Chef Nak, the gold standard of Cambodian cookbooks → https://amzn.to/4gt8761
  ◦ Angkor: Celestial Temples of the Khmer Empire by Jon Ortner, the coffee table photography volume → https://amzn.to/3SP3Yzt

comments.author: @zxas-z5w
comments.text: 吴哥窟，在中国元代典籍《真腊风土记》已经记载了关于当时吴哥窟的一切，包括风俗，生活方式，城市布局，等等，《真腊风土记》由元朝人周达观于13世纪末访问吴哥王国后撰写而成

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: 谢谢补充这个重要的历史来源 — 《真腊风土记》确实是了解吴哥王朝当时生活方式最珍贵的第一手记录之一。

comments.author: @ChanelNatureTravel4K
comments.text: It's amazing to think that one of the largest cities on Earth could disappear beneath the jungle and remain hidden for centuries. The scale of Angkor is far beyond the famous temples most people know. Beneath the forest lies the remains of a vast urban network that once supported hundreds of thousands of people. This documentary does a fantastic job revealing just how advanced and extraordinary this lost civilization really was.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thank you — that scale is easy to undersell until you see how far the urban network actually extended beyond the temples people know. Glad it came through.

comments.author: @davidreynolds4943
comments.text: Very refreshing to see a new documentary in 2026 thoughtfully written with excellent filming, based on real science.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thank you — really glad the research side came through as clearly as the visuals.

comments.author: @trueunrealglobe
comments.text: The narration perfectly complemented the visuals and helped create a captivating travel experience.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thank you — glad the two worked together the way we hoped.

comments.author: @khomiskhmer
comments.text: As a young Khmer person, I am very proud of Angkor Wat. Besides studying, I spend most of my free time researching Angkor Wat and the knowledge that our Khmer ancestors built.Thank you for your video🙌💗

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That kind of pride and curiosity is exactly what keeps this history alive for the next generation. Thank you for watching, and for carrying that forward yourself.

replies.author: @trueunrealglobe
replies.text: This is exactly the kind of travel content that inspires people to explore beyond their comfort zones.

comments.author: @TRAORÉSBRAVERY
comments.text: An old Khmer legend tells of a curse placed on the first Angkor king after a grave injustice. The prophecy said the kingdom would one day decline and its great cities would be swallowed by the jungle. While only a myth, it is remarkable how Angkor was eventually abandoned and reclaimed by nature. Today, the story remains a reminder of the importance of justice, unity, and remembering our roots.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: A curse about justice and unity outlasting the empire itself — that's exactly the kind of story that makes Angkor so much more than a ruin. Thank you for watching. 🌿

comments.author: @TraTourguide
comments.text: Very informative and detailed thanks for your documentary.

comments.author: @NomadicCorner
comments.text: Angkor is more than an ancient temple complex. It is the heart of a once-powerful empire, where magnificent stone temples, vast reservoirs, and centuries of history remain hidden within the forests of Cambodia

comments.author: @angkorkhmerempire6079
comments.text: I am amazed and grateful that youtube and history channels still spend so much times and energy spreading the Khmer Empire to the world all the time even though it so saturated. The Khmer Empire and the temple building projects, the engineering, the astrology, the hydraulic city and continue to amaze from the ordinary people all the way to historians and archaeologists. Whoever makes these videos, we appreciate your times and efforts. We are grateful for all  the videos and keep as a reminder that we were once the greatest civilization on the planet. Empires come and go, the Khmer Empire was unfortunate and will never recover, but as long as we don't forget our roots and united, we will overcome any obstacles.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That thread — the hydraulic city, the temples, the engineering — still holds even when the empire doesn't. Thank you for watching. 🌿

comments.author: @sammpen4149
comments.text:
It is said in an old Khmer mythology an old ascetic being put a devastating curse on the first Angkor king because the king sinfully caused one of his student to perish in agony in a giant boiling cauldron. 
According to the curses the king and his royal subjects and commoners will live in suffering and dominated by foreigners, and all the cities will turn into jungles. 
This myth sounds unrealistic but it seems over the thousands of years it did came true! To this days Khmer bloodline are thinning out and Khmer kingdom has shrunk dramatically. Nowadays the Thai, the Vietcong are on Khmer land and controlling most of the territories. Even Laos which was once part of the Angkor kingdom it is now break up into another country but to this day they’re also controlled by Vietnam. This is not a made up myth cuz it was taught by our old grand Buddhist monks long ago but I only summed it up in a nutshell.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That curse legend is a striking piece of oral history — the idea of myth tracking so closely with centuries of real decline says a lot about how the Khmer people made sense of their own story. Thank you for sharing it in such detail.

comments.author: @EpicAdventureWorld
comments.text: Everyone talks about the temples, but I think the engineering behind Angkor is even more incredible.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Completely agree — the hydraulic network alone rivals anything else from that era. What part of the engineering stood out to you most?

replies.author: @khomiskhmer
replies.text: Yes, as a Khmer, I never saw Angkor Wat simply as a temple, but also as a place of mathematical knowledge, spirituality, and astrology.
